content.text: Data Pipelines in Python — Pipeline Design Patterns
A data pipeline is a sequence of processing steps where data flows from a source, through transformations, to a destination. The

chosen for that pipeline determines how steps are connected, how data moves between them, how errors are handled, and how the system scales under load. Choosing the right pattern is one of the most consequential architectural decisions in any data-intensive Python application.

A pipeline design pattern is a reusable structural template for organizing how data moves and gets transformed through a series of stages. Rather than writing ad-hoc transformation logic in one giant block, patterns impose structure: each stage has a clear responsibility, defined inputs and outputs, and a predictable way of connecting to the next stage.
In Python, these patterns are implemented using generators, iterators, queues, async primitives, functional composition, and frameworks. The same core concept —
— can be expressed in dozens of structurally distinct ways, each with different trade-offs around throughput, latency, memory, complexity, and fault tolerance.
Without a deliberate design pattern, data pipelines quickly become:
— all logic entangled, impossible to test in isolation
— loading entire datasets into RAM before processing
— adding a new step requires touching unrelated code
— no clear stage boundaries to distribute across workers
— when something breaks, it is impossible to tell which stage failed and why

A well-chosen pattern makes pipelines modular, testable, memory-efficient, and observable. It also acts as a shared vocabulary: when a team says "this is a fan-out fan-in pattern," everyone immediately understands the data flow topology.
The patterns below are organized from simplest to most sophisticated. Most real-world pipelines combine two or more of these.
The simplest pattern. Data flows through a fixed ordered sequence of stages, one at a time, synchronously. Each stage receives the output of the previous one.
An assembly line — a car chassis moves from welding → painting → quality check → packaging, one step at a time.

def extract(source: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Stage 1: Read raw data"""
    import json
    with open(source) as f:
        return json.load(f)

def transform(records: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    """Stage 2: Clean and reshape"""
    return [
        {
            "id": r["user_id"],
            "name": r["name"].strip().title(),
            "age": int(r["age"]),
        }
        for r in records
        if r.get("age") and int(r["age"]) >= 18
    ]

def load(records: list[dict], dest: str) -> None:
    """Stage 3: Write to destination"""
    import json
    with open(dest, "w") as f:
        json.dump(records, f, indent=2)

# Execute the pipeline
raw = extract("users.json")
cleaned = transform(raw)
load(cleaned, "users_clean.json")
Entire dataset held in memory at each stage boundary
No parallelism — stages run one after another
Any stage failure aborts the whole run
Small datasets, scripts, prototyping, ETL jobs where simplicity trumps throughput.

Stages are implemented as Python generators. Data flows one record (or one chunk) at a time — each item is pulled through all stages before the next item enters. Memory usage is nearly constant regardless of dataset size.
A conveyor belt where items flow one-by-one. The belt only moves when the consumer at the end pulls.
, which reads one line from the file. The entire chain is lazy. Python's generator protocol (
O(1) memory — processes billions of records with constant RAM
Composable — stages are plain functions, easy to swap
Streaming-friendly — output can start appearing before input is fully consumed
Single-threaded — no parallelism within the chain
Generators are stateful and not reusable (once exhausted, they cannot be rewound)
Backpressure is implicit — the consumer sets the pace
Large file processing, streaming APIs, log ingestion, any scenario where loading the full dataset into memory is impractical.
Stages are pure functions composed together using higher-order functions. The pipeline is expressed as a series of
Pure functions are trivially testable in isolation
The pipeline definition reads like a declarative specification
Easy to insert, remove, or reorder stages
Requires discipline to keep functions truly pure (no side effects)
Debugging generator chains can be harder — errors surface at consumption time
Not a natural fit for stages that need to accumulate state (e.g., deduplication, windowed aggregations)
Data transformation logic, ETL transformations, analytics pipelines, anywhere testability and composability are priorities.

Stages run as separate threads (or processes), communicating through thread-safe queues. Producers put items on the queue; consumers take items off. This enables true parallel execution across stages.

A restaurant kitchen — one chef (producer) prepares dishes and puts them on a pass-through shelf (queue); another chef (consumer) takes them and plates them. Both work simultaneously and independently.

import threading
import queue
import json
import time
from typing import Callable

SENTINEL = object()  # Poison pill to signal end of stream

def producer(filepath: str, out_queue: queue.Queue) -> None:
    """Read records and push to queue"""
    with open(filepath) as f:
        for line in f:
            record = json.loads(line)
            out_queue.put(record)
    out_queue.put(SENTINEL)  # Signal completion
    print("Producer done")

def transformer(
    in_queue: queue.Queue,
    out_queue: queue.Queue,
    transform_fn: Callable,
) -> None:
    """Pull from input queue, transform, push to output queue"""
    while True:
        item = in_queue.get()
        if item is SENTINEL:
            out_queue.put(SENTINEL)  # Propagate sentinel downstream
            break
        try:
            result = transform_fn(item)
            if result is not None:
                out_queue.put(result)
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Transform error: {e}, skipping record")
        finally:
            in_queue.task_done()
    print("Transformer done")

def consumer(in_queue: queue.Queue, output_file: str) -> None:
    """Drain queue and write to destination"""
    count = 0
    with open(output_file, "w") as f:
        while True:
            item = in_queue.get()
            if item is SENTINEL:
                break
            f.write(json.dumps(item) + "\n")
            count += 1
            in_queue.task_done()
    print(f"Consumer done: wrote {count} records")

# Transformation logic
def clean_record(r: dict) -> dict | None:
    age = int(r.get("age", 0))
    if age < 18:
        return None  # Filter out
    return {
        "id": r["user_id"],
        "name": r["name"].strip().title(),
        "age": age,
    }

# Wire up the pipeline
q1 = queue.Queue(maxsize=100)  # Backpressure: blocks producer if full
q2 = queue.Queue(maxsize=100)

t1 = threading.Thread(target=producer, args=("users.jsonl", q1))
t2 = threading.Thread(target=transformer, args=(q1, q2, clean_record))
t3 = threading.Thread(target=consumer, args=(q2, "output.jsonl"))

# Start all stages simultaneously
for t in (t1, t2, t3):
    t.start()

for t in (t1, t2, t3):
    t.join()
on each queue is critical. When the queue is full,
blocks. This prevents a fast producer from overwhelming a slow consumer and consuming unbounded memory.
True parallelism — all stages run simultaneously in separate threads
Stages can be independently scaled (multiple transformer threads)
GIL limits CPU-bound parallelism in threads; use
Complexity increases significantly — deadlocks, sentinel propagation, error handling
Debugging is harder — failures in one thread do not automatically propagate
I/O-bound pipelines (network fetching, file I/O) where stages have different speeds; pipelines that benefit from concurrent execution.
One stage splits work across multiple parallel workers (fan-out), then a downstream stage aggregates the results (fan-in). The canonical use case is CPU-bound parallel processing.
A document scanning operation — one loader reads pages and distributes them to 8 OCR workers (fan-out); a merger collects the OCR results and assembles the final document (fan-in).

import concurrent.futures
import json
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Callable

def process_chunk(chunk: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    """CPU-bound transformation applied to a chunk of records"""
    results = []
    for record in chunk:
        # Simulate expensive computation
        processed = {
            "id": record["user_id"],
            "score": sum(ord(c) for c in record["name"]),  # Placeholder
            "age": int(record["age"]),
        }
        results.append(processed)
    return results

def chunked(iterable, size: int):
    """Split an iterable into chunks of given size"""
    chunk = []
    for item in iterable:
        chunk.append(item)
        if len(chunk) == size:
            yield chunk
            chunk = []
    if chunk:
        yield chunk

def fan_out_fan_in_pipeline(
    source_file: str,
    output_file: str,
    worker_fn: Callable,
    chunk_size: int = 500,
    max_workers: int = 4,
) -> int:
    """
    Fan-out: distribute chunks to worker pool
    Fan-in: collect results and write output
    """
    with open(source_file) as f:
        records = [json.loads(line) for line in f]

    chunks = list(chunked(records, chunk_size))
    print(f"Fan-out: {len(chunks)} chunks to {max_workers} workers")

    all_results = []

    # ProcessPoolExecutor bypasses the GIL for true CPU parallelism
    with concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=max_workers) as executor:
        # Submit all chunks and get Future objects
        futures = {executor.submit(worker_fn, chunk): i
                   for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks)}

        # Fan-in: collect results as they complete
        for future in concurrent.futures.as_completed(futures):
            chunk_idx = futures[future]
            try:
                result = future.result()
                all_results.extend(result)
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"Chunk {chunk_idx} failed: {e}")

    print(f"Fan-in: collected {len(all_results)} results")

    with open(output_file, "w") as f:
        for record in all_results:
            f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")

    return len(all_results)

count = fan_out_fan_in_pipeline(
    "users.jsonl",
    "output.jsonl",
    process_chunk,
    chunk_size=500,
    max_workers=4,
)
Throughput scales (nearly) linearly with worker count for CPU-bound work
Spawning processes has overhead — not worth it for fast or tiny jobs
Output order is not guaranteed without explicit ordering logic
Workers must be picklable (no lambdas, no unpicklable objects)
Memory usage multiplies with worker count
CPU-bound transformations (ML inference on batches, image processing, text encoding), large datasets that can be cleanly partitioned.
Stages are async coroutines; data flows through
objects. The entire pipeline runs on a single event loop thread, but I/O waits are non-blocking, allowing many operations to be in-flight simultaneously.

A single barista who takes an order, starts the espresso machine, while it runs takes the next order, starts steaming milk, while that runs hands off the completed first order — never blocking, always doing something useful.

import asyncio
import json
import aiohttp
import aiofiles
from typing import AsyncIterator

async def async_producer(
    urls: list[str],
    out_queue: asyncio.Queue,
    concurrency: int = 10,
) -> None:
    """Fetch many URLs concurrently and push results to queue"""
    semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency)  # Limit concurrent requests

    async def fetch_one(session: aiohttp.ClientSession, url: str) -> dict:
        async with semaphore:
            async with session.get(url, timeout=aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=10)) as resp:
                data = await resp.json()
                return {"url": url, "data": data, "status": resp.status}

    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch_one(session, url) for url in urls]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

    for result in results:
        if isinstance(result, Exception):
            print(f"Fetch error: {result}")
        else:
            await out_queue.put(result)

    await out_queue.put(None)  # Sentinel

async def async_transformer(
    in_queue: asyncio.Queue,
    out_queue: asyncio.Queue,
) -> None:
    """Transform records — could call an async LLM API here"""
    while True:
        item = await in_queue.get()
        if item is None:
            await out_queue.put(None)
            return

        # Simulate async transformation (e.g., async DB lookup)
        await asyncio.sleep(0)  # Yield control
        transformed = {
            "source": item["url"],
            "record_count": len(item.get("data", [])),
            "ok": item["status"] == 200,
        }
        await out_queue.put(transformed)

async def async_consumer(
    in_queue: asyncio.Queue,
    output_file: str,
) -> int:
    """Write results to file asynchronously"""
    count = 0
    async with aiofiles.open(output_file, "w") as f:
        while True:
            item = await in_queue.get()
            if item is None:
                break
            await f.write(json.dumps(item) + "\n")
            count += 1
    return count

async def run_async_pipeline(urls: list[str], output: str) -> None:
    q1: asyncio.Queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=50)
    q2: asyncio.Queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=50)

    await asyncio.gather(
        async_producer(urls, q1),
        async_transformer(q1, q2),
        async_consumer(q2, output),
    )

# Entry point
asyncio.run(run_async_pipeline(
    urls=["https://api.example.com/users/" + str(i) for i in range(1000)],
    output="output.jsonl",
))
Extremely high I/O concurrency on a single thread — thousands of concurrent network requests
Low memory per concurrent operation (coroutines are cheap vs threads)
Does not help with CPU-bound work — one thread, no parallelism there
syntax propagates through the entire codebase (viral)
Mixing sync and async code requires
Debugging and error propagation requires care
API-heavy pipelines (LLM APIs, REST services), web scraping, database pipelines using asyncpg, any pipeline where the bottleneck is I/O wait time.
Records are routed to different downstream stages based on their content. Rather than one linear chain, the pipeline has conditional branches that lead to different destinations or transformations.
A postal sorting machine — parcels enter on one belt and are routed to different bins based on postal code. Each bin leads to a different truck.
Clean separation of routing logic from transformation logic
New routes can be added without touching existing handlers
Records can be sent to multiple destinations with a slight variation (multicast)
Harder to reason about end-to-end data flow when branching is complex
Shared state between branches requires careful synchronization
Pattern 8: Pipeline with Checkpointing / Resumable Pipeline
The pipeline periodically saves its progress to durable storage (a checkpoint). If the pipeline fails mid-run, it can resume from the last checkpoint rather than reprocessing from the beginning.
A video game save point. If you die, you restart from the save, not from the beginning of the game.
Long-running pipelines (hours), pipelines over unreliable data sources, expensive transformations where reprocessing is costly.
The diagram below illustrates how each pattern maps to a different data flow topology:

LINEAR:
  [Source] ──► [Stage A] ──► [Stage B] ──► [Stage C] ──► [Sink]

GENERATOR (LAZY):
  [Source] ──► [gen A] ──► [gen B] ──► [gen C] ──► consumer pulls

PRODUCER-CONSUMER (QUEUES):
  [Thread: Producer] ──► Q1 ──► [Thread: Transformer] ──► Q2 ──► [Thread: Consumer]

FAN-OUT / FAN-IN:
                       ┌──► [Worker 1] ──┐
  [Source] ──► [Split] ├──► [Worker 2] ──┼──► [Merge] ──► [Sink]
                       └──► [Worker 3] ──┘

BRANCHING:
                              ┌──► [Handler: US]    ──► [Sink A]
  [Source] ──► [Router] ──────┼──► [Handler: Intl]  ──► [Sink B]
                              └──► [Handler: Minor] ──► [Drop]

ASYNC (I/O BOUND):
  [Coroutine: Fetch] ──► asyncio.Queue ──► [Coroutine: Transform]
    (1000 concurrent I/O waits on one thread)
Heavy I/O, thousands of API calls
Real-world pipelines are almost always hybrids. A production LLM data pipeline might combine:
for streaming millions of source records from S3
to distribute embedding generation across GPU workers
to batch-call the OpenAI embeddings API concurrently
to route records with errors to a dead-letter queue
to resume after API rate-limit failures

# Sketch of a hybrid pipeline

async def hybrid_pipeline(source_path: str, checkpoint: Checkpoint):
    # Layer 1: Lazy generator reads source
    records = stream_from_s3(source_path)

    # Layer 2: Chunk for batch processing
    chunks = chunked(records, size=100)

    # Layer 3: Fan-out to async workers
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        for chunk in chunks:
            tg.create_task(process_chunk_async(chunk, checkpoint))
before passing it to the next stage defeats the entire purpose of lazy evaluation and can exhaust memory on large datasets.
In queue-based pipelines, every stage must forward the sentinel value downstream. If a transformer consumes the sentinel but forgets to put it on the outbound queue, the consumer will block forever.
Python's GIL means threads do not provide true parallelism for CPU-bound code. Use
or move work to a C extension (numpy, etc.) for genuine CPU parallelism.
allows unlimited memory growth if the producer is faster than the consumer. Always set
in a pipeline stage hides data quality problems and makes it impossible to know how many records were silently dropped. Always log or count errors.
can result in a state where the checkpoint claims N records were written but the file only has N-100 (buffered and not yet on disk).
Each stage should have an explicit input type and output type. Use Python type hints (
A generator-based stage can be tested by calling
. A queue-based stage can be tested with a pre-filled
. Never require the full pipeline to run a unit test.

Add counters (records in, records out, errors) to every stage. Emit them as structured logs or metrics. Without this, a pipeline that silently drops 10% of records looks identical to one that processes 100%.
objects between stages allows schema drift — one stage writes
classes for inter-stage data to catch mismatches at definition time.
Whether through bounded queues, semaphores in async code, or chunk size limits, always give downstream stages the ability to signal that they are overwhelmed.
Separate the pipeline definition from execution.
Build the pipeline as a composition of stages, then execute it. This makes it easy to test the structure, swap stages, or add middleware (logging, timing, retries) without modifying stage logic.
A pipeline that reads 10 million product descriptions from a database, generates vector embeddings via the OpenAI API, and writes them to a vector store would use:
Stream rows from the database using
cursor, yielding one row at a time
Batch records into groups of 100 (OpenAI's embedding API accepts up to 2048 tokens per batch)
submits up to 20 batches concurrently to the API (controlled by a semaphore to respect rate limits)
Successful batches go to the vector store writer; API errors go to a retry queue with exponential backoff
Every 10,000 records, save the last processed row ID to a Redis key so the pipeline can resume after a crash
This hybrid architecture processes ~10,000 embeddings per second at steady state while remaining resumable, memory-efficient, and observable through per-stage counters.
should be the default for any Python pipeline — it is memory-efficient, composable, and idiomatic
are the right tool when stages have unequal speed and need to run concurrently
provides the only true CPU parallelism in Python; async provides I/O concurrency, not CPU parallelism
(bounded queues, semaphores) is not optional — without it, fast producers will crash the pipeline under load
turns a fragile long-running job into a reliable, resumable one
Every pattern is more effective when stages have typed contracts, isolated tests, and per-stage observability
Most production pipelines are hybrids — choose the dominant pattern for the bottleneck stage, then layer in supporting patterns for the rest
